title: Leadership Resilience Wheel
tagline: Eight markers of resilience under AI-era pressure. See your wheel in two minutes.

children.text: Eight domains, eight sliders. Rate yourself where you are right now — not where you usually are, not where you'd like to be. The result is a wheel you can keep.

questions.prompt: Right now, how easily do you keep going on hard things over weeks and months?
questions.anchorLow: I give up sooner than I'd like
questions.anchorHigh: I stay with it until it's done

questions.prompt: How easily do you adjust when the plan, the tools, or the role itself shifts under you?
questions.anchorHigh: I adapt fast and find the new shape

questions.prompt: How much do you trust your own judgement at the moment?
questions.anchorHigh: I trust what I see and decide

questions.prompt: How much patience and care do you have available for the people you lead right now — without it draining you?
How easily can you find lightness — in yourself, your situation, your work?
questions.anchorHigh: I can still laugh, even at the hard stuff

questions.prompt: How clearly are you seeing your situation — not catastrophising, not glossing over, not waiting for it to settle?
questions.anchorLow: I'm distorting it in one direction or another
questions.anchorHigh: I'm seeing it for what it is

questions.prompt: How well are the basics — sleep, movement, eating, time off — actually holding up?
How present and active are your close relationships right now?
questions.anchorLow: I'm isolated, even if I don't always feel it
questions.anchorHigh: I have real, regular contact with people who know me

dimensions.label: Perseverance
dimensions.description: Keeping going on hard things over time.

dimensions.label: Adaptive Capacity
dimensions.description: The structural ability to absorb change without breaking.

dimensions.label: Compassion
dimensions.description: Care for others that doesn't deplete you.

dimensions.label: Clear-Eyed
dimensions.description: Seeing the situation as it actually is.

dimensions.label: Balance
dimensions.description: The basics holding up: sleep, movement, food, time off.

dimensions.label: Connection
dimensions.description: Real, active closeness with people who know you.

children.text: You're in surplus. That's worth noticing — most senior leaders aren't, right now. The question for you is what you do with the surplus. Resources at rest tend to find a use, and not always the one you'd choose.

Functioning, but with thinning margins in places. Your two weakest domains are below — worth sitting with them rather than averaging them out against the strong ones. The strong ones aren't the question.

You're holding it together but it's costing you. That cost is rarely paid where it shows up — usually it lands on the people closest to you and on the parts of your work that depend on judgement rather than execution. Worth getting honest about which it is.

This isn't a coaching question yet — it's a recovery question. Most of the work senior leaders try to do from here doesn't take, because the soil isn't ready. The useful next step is rest and recovery, not strategy.

The question to sit with: is this a thing you've genuinely lost the will for, or a thing you've been running too hot on for too long? The answer changes the next move. One asks for honesty about purpose; the other asks for permission to rest.

You may be running on a model of your role that no longer fits the actual conditions. This is the dominant resilience question of the current moment. The work isn't more grit — it's deciding which parts of how you operate are still load-bearing, and which ones you're carrying out of habit.

Confidence rarely comes back through pep talks. It comes back through small completed things — a finished piece of work, a hard conversation handled, a decision made and held. What's the smallest one available this week?

You're likely running on empty for yourself first, others second. The order matters. Refilling the first usually fixes the second; trying to give from a depleted reserve usually just deepens the depletion.
When humour goes, it usually means something serious has gone unspoken. What is it? The fix here is rarely "lighten up" — it's naming whatever's been carried in silence.

Two common patterns sit underneath a low score here. One: you're catastrophising about a specific area of your work — often the area most exposed to AI or restructure. Two: you're glossing over something obvious because looking at it directly is inconvenient. Worth checking which it is. Honest leaders can hold either pattern; that's not the question. The question is whether you've noticed.

This isn't a willpower problem. The basics are infrastructure. They need maintenance, not motivation. Senior leaders who treat sleep, movement and downtime as discretionary tend to discover — usually badly — that they aren't.

Three kinds of isolation are common at senior level right now. Isolation from people you used to confide in, because their context has diverged from yours. Isolation from your own team, because the role asks you to hold things you can't share. Isolation that comes from doing most of your thinking with tools rather than people. Worth being specific about which one this is.
Want your wheel as a PDF, plus the seven reflection prompts that go with it?

seoDescription: Eight markers of leadership resilience under AI-era pressure. See your wheel in two minutes. Honest, visual, immediate.
